justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California mandatory transparency/AB5, Germany works council gate, Indonesia optional/no legal mandate. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition (BR-5, AC-3). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee (CA, Germany) vs independent contractor (Indonesia) with effects on payroll fields and rules. (4) Formal escalations for legal review present across multiple escalation entries. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with 10 well-differentiated business rules covering all three jurisdictions
BR-5 correctly identifies works council approval as a hard gate with feature flag enforcement, not just a nice-to-have
Driver classification difference clearly articulated — employee vs contractor affects which wage laws, deduction rules, and transparency obligations apply
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects legal complexity and multiple unknowns
Catches the subtle trap that California mandatory display cannot be removed globally — BR-4 makes this explicit as an invariant
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as necessary, with California and Germany flagged as HIGH risk
Edge cases are strong — covers cross-jurisdiction delivery routing, works council modifications, and minimum wage floor enforcement
Multiple formal escalations with structured options and recommendations for legal review, works council engagement, and §226 compliance
Ubiquitous language includes Driver Classification and Works Council with precise jurisdiction-aware definitions

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with 10 business rules, each tagged with invariant status and legal justification
Works Council requirement correctly identified as a hard gate with specific legal citation (BetrVG §87) and enforcement via feature flag + approval record
Driver classification difference explicitly called out in ubiquitous language and woven through rules (employee in CA/DE vs contractor in ID)
Correctly identified the subtle trap: removing transparency in CA would be illegal even if optional elsewhere (implicit in BR-3's invariant status)
Edge cases are excellent — Works Council revocation, driver relocation mid-period, cross-jurisdiction deliveries all addressed
Business impact correctly identifies phased rollout necessity and quantifies CA violation penalties ($50-$4,000 per violation under LC §226)
Confidence level of 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility
Three structured escalations with options and recommendations — covers real-time definition ambiguity, DE classification confirmation, and deduction taxonomy gap

(2) Germany Works Council approval identified as a blocking pre-condition with feature gate enforcement (BR-4 marked invariant, AC-2 blocks access). (4) Formal escalations for legal review present across multiple escalation entries. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — jurisdiction-specific ACs (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for Germany, AC-4 for Indonesia). (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with distinct acceptance criteria per jurisdiction and classification-aware data model rules.
Works Council approval correctly modeled as an invariant with feature gate enforcement, not just a soft recommendation.
Driver classification difference (employee vs. contractor) explicitly called out as a data integrity invariant (BR-5) with concrete testable assertion showing different component sets.

Multiple formal escalations covering real-time SLA ambiguity, Works Council revocation, California §226 field requirements, and payroll reconciliation — all with structured options and recommendations.
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform, noting jurisdiction-aware feature gating and classification-aware templates.
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects legal complexity with well-articulated low-confidence areas.
Edge cases are excellent — covers reclassification on relocation, Works Council approval revocation, negative net earnings, and currency conversion scenarios.
Correctly identifies the subtle trap: tip handling rules in California (BR-7) that cannot be relaxed globally, and misclassification signal risk in business_impact.
